div.head: HO–22. Memorandum from the Secretary of State to the PresidentSource: Eisenhower Library, Whitman File, Dulles-Herter Series. Confidential.

list.head: SUBJECT
list.item: Submission of the Dispute with Honduras concerning Sovereignty over Swan Islands to the International Court of Justice.

div.p: A long-standing dispute between the United States and Honduras concerning sovereignty over two small Caribbean islands, the Swan Islands, is now becoming so inflamed in Honduras that it threatens U.S.–Honduran relations, and even the stability of the moderate administration of President Villeda Morales. For almost 100 years, the islands have been occupied by United States citizens and utilized by United States interests. The Cuban Government, in its general propaganda campaign against the United States, has been agitating the dispute throughout the hemisphere, partly to aggravate an issue between the United States and Honduras, and partly because of anti-Castro broadcasts being made by a commercial radio station on one of the islands.

The Hondurans have convinced themselves that their claim to these islands is unassailable. However, the U.S. has long claimed the islands, and after careful study, we believe the U.S. has the better claim.
A memorandum discussing more fully the dispute over sovereignty is enclosed.Not printed.

There is no way to resolve this issue by compromise, since each side believes that it has sovereignty. The only method by which it can now be resolved, in my judgment, is to have the question of sovereignty decided by an impartial tribunal, the International Court of Justice. High officials of the Honduran Government have given indications that Honduras would probably agree to this course. A statement regarding the relevance of the Connelly AmendmentOn August 2, 1946, the United States Senate passed Senate Resolution 196 enabling the United States under certain conditions, to accept the jurisdiction of the International Court of Justice. In the course of the Senate’s consideration of this resolution, Sen. Tom Connally (D.-Tex.) obtained the adoption of an amendment to the resolution that permitted the United States to reserve the right to exempt all disputes from the ICJ’s compulsory jurisdiction that involved the domestic jurisdiction of the United States and to determine the definition of the term “domestic jurisdiction.” For the text of Senate Resolution 196, 79th Cong., 2nd Sess., see Foreign Relations, 1946, vol. I, General; The United Nations, Document 34.. is enclosed.[The text of footnote 4 is missing in the source text.]

It is likely that the United States will win the case if it is submitted. However, even if this were not the situation, I believe it would still be highly desirable for the United States to manifest, particularly in its relations with a small country such as Honduras, its readiness in appropriate cases to accept the resolution of a dispute by an impartial tribunal in accordance with international law.

Submission of the Swan Islands dispute to the International Court of Justice appears to offer the best available course of action for the United States in its relations with Honduras, and it has been the policy of the United States to support and foster the peaceful settlement of disputes by the International
Court of Justice in appropriate cases. The Department of Defense and the Central Intelligence Agency have no objection to such a course of action.

I request that you authorize me to seek agreement with the Government of Honduras for the submission of this dispute to the International Court of Justice.According to a handwritten notation on a carbon copy of the source text, the Department of State was authorized on December 9, 1960, to proceed with this course of action. (Eisenhower Library, Project Clean-Up)
